confidence.disclosure: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Baqir, the fifth Twelver Shia imam, is documented in the major Shia biographical works (al-Kulayni's al-Kafi, al-Mufid's Kitab al-Irshad) and in Sunni hadith and biographical literature, where he is recognised as a major early traditionist. His birth in Medina in 56 or 57 AH, his education under his father Ali Zayn al-Abidin, his accession as imam in 95 AH, his teaching career in Medina through the late Umayyad period, and his death in 114 or 117 AH in Medina are firmly attested in outline. The exact dates are reported with some variation; this journey follows the date most commonly given in the early sources.

sources: Encyclopaedia of Islam, 2nd edition, 'Muhammad b. Ali Zayn al-Abidin'

journey.event: Born in Medina around 1 Rajab 57 AH (3 May 677), the son of the fourth imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (Zayn al-Abidin) and Fatima bint al-Hasan ibn Ali. He is therefore descended from both al-Hasan and al-Husayn, the only imam of the Twelver line so descended through both lines. The Prophet himself is said to have foretold his birth and given him the title al-Baqir ('he who splits open' the knowledge of religion).

On 10 Muharram 61 AH (10 October 680), as a child of about three, is present at Karbala when his great-uncle al-Husayn and many of his relatives are killed by the Umayyad army. Survives because of his youth, returning to Medina with the captives via Damascus.

Reared in Medina by his father Ali Zayn al-Abidin, who teaches him the Quran, the family traditions of the Prophet, and the supplications of the Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya. Also studies hadith from his great-uncle Jabir ibn Abd Allah al-Ansari, the famous companion who lives long enough to transmit the Prophet's traditions to the third Muslim generation.

Continues his religious education and begins to teach in Medina alongside his father. Becomes known as a major hadith transmitter and is consulted by Sunni and Shia scholars alike, including the great early jurists.

On the death of his father Ali Zayn al-Abidin in 95 AH (713 CE), succeeds him as fifth imam of the Husaynid line. Like his father, adopts a strictly quietist political stance, refusing to associate himself with any of the Alid risings against the Umayyads and concentrating his energies on teaching.

Establishes a large teaching circle in the Prophet's Mosque in Medina. Among his students are many of the leading Kufan hadith scholars and several of the founders of the early legal schools, including the future imam of his line Jafar al-Sadiq (his own son). His circle also includes Abu Hanifa, who reportedly studies with him during pilgrimages to Medina.

Articulates many of the doctrines that will become foundational to Twelver Shia theology and law: the doctrine of taqiyya (precautionary dissimulation under persecution), the principle that the imam is the divinely-designated authoritative interpreter of the Quran, and the rejection of analogical reasoning (qiyas) in favour of strict reliance on the texts and the imam's teaching. The legal and theological positions transmitted from him form a major part of the corpus of early imami doctrine.

Around this period is summoned at least once to Damascus by the Umayyad caliph Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik, who is suspicious of his growing reputation. According to Shia tradition, the encounter ends with the imam confounding the caliph and his religious advisers in debate and being permitted to return to Medina unharmed. The story may be partly hagiographical but the underlying summons is plausible given Umayyad surveillance of the Alids.

In his last years devotes himself to training his son Jafar al-Sadiq as his successor, formally transmitting to him the imamate and the body of teachings of the family. Under Jafar the Husaynid line will reach the height of its intellectual influence, but the foundation laid by al-Baqir is decisive.

Dies in Medina in 114 AH (733 CE), aged about 57. Shia tradition holds that he was poisoned at the instigation of an Umayyad agent, but the early Sunni sources record his death as natural. Buried in al-Baqi cemetery in Medina alongside his father Ali Zayn al-Abidin, his uncle al-Hasan ibn Ali, and many of the family of the Prophet. Succeeded by his son Jafar al-Sadiq as sixth imam of the Twelver line. His grave at al-Baqi remained a place of pilgrimage for centuries.
